speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five. I'm interviewing Mr. Allan Goddard. Mr. Goddard, you were telling me that you had one son who was born in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you brought him to Australia. Did you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Stephen was born here in 1956, exactly 10 years younger than Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does Michael do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael went through high school with distinctions. He also finished his studying in University of Monash and prepared for a bachelorship and also worked on his thesis. But for some reasons, he didn't finish that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is Stephen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephen went also through the high school, not with distinction, but already good results. He went first to the university. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And now he's preparing also for a master. But he's now employed by the University of Deakin. He's a senior lecturer. He has his class preparing people for future occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But he has got a fiance. And when they will finish studies, they decided to marry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Goddard, after 50 years since the war finished, have you got any special message that you would like to make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was around Pesach, somewhere-- March, April. I knew that the 50th anniversary of our liberation is approaching. And we shouldn't let go this date without any particular celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I contacted our friends here in Melbourne. And they agreed that we will make a dinner in Kimberly Gardens, where we invited the girl and the photographer from the and from the

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a very nice evening. We've made some photographs. And we had all discussions by that time. But I wasn't already set with that one. I contacted the secretary of the [INAUDIBLE] Melbourne radio station, or ABC. I provided a script with my story. And it was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next day, on Monday, the seventh of May, I was interviewed by Ronald MacDonald. And I told him my story, because at the same time, we had here in Melbourne the man who started the whole story.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? We will ?] call him Leopold [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Page. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was with his friends here in Melbourne. When I mentioned that, the secretary, she said, bring him, also. We will make with him the interview. But he was so booked out. And the next day, he had to return to America. But I knew where he was. He gave me his telephone number. And I mentioned, the secretary said, OK, we will interview him by phone. So when my interview finished, Ronald called for Leopold Page, and he has done his part. At the end, he asked him when the tape will be ready to give it to me and send it to him to America. So I have the tape of that commemoration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you made any decisions, or have you done anything that was influenced by your Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I was approached by one man from the Australian Unity, or, by that time, it was probably Manchester Unity, when he heard that I'm one of the survivors, if I would spare some time to give us some idea how it was during that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was invited to a group of Australian older ones, younger ones. And I had a rad of that script, which was very nice, accepted. That was my first contact with the non-Jewish community. Apart from that, I only had a chance to talk about that in my synagogue. When they nominated me as a Chatan Torah, I had a chance to give them the idea how this was during the Holocaust. That was all what I can say. Has it affected how you feel about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strongly, always, underlying-- I was never in doubt about it because I'm [INAUDIBLE]. So that makes me different from other people. And say, for our argument, if our synagogue has got 300 members, there were only a dozen or so, a handful of people, who were blessing during the high holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph represents my mother when she was young, when she was met by my father, before the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is that in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother when we left concentration camp in 1945, that was the photo taken for her passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife and myself after we left the concentration camp and were registered in the former-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] in the former camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: We married in Krakow on the 21st of March, 1941. Of course, our photos were all destroyed in Gross-Rosen when we had to leave all our belongings. So when reunited after the war, we decided to make a replica of our wedding photo. And this is what we made in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me who these people are?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, [? Nugia, ?] with her husband, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who divorced. They were divorced later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our first photo when Michael was born in Bielsko in 1946, before we went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my photo and my younger brothers, which we made soon after Michael was born in Bielsko. The second after me is Joseph. The last one is Henry. Unfortunately, both passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is that, please, in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger brother, Joseph, married in Israel during our stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is that in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my sons. First on the right is Michael, nursing his brother Stephen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael, a photo when we went to a holiday home some time ago. And this is Stephen, who was together with us in the same moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael during the graduation in the Monash University. This is Stephen's graduation in the University [INAUDIBLE] Latrobe, yes, in the Latrobe University.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a happy couple after the 50 years of a happy reunion [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the 21st of March, 1955-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] 56, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate that, after we arrived to Gross-Rosen, and we changed our clothing to stripe, a number was sewn on our left side-- 69516.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you please tell me who is sitting with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Fela. And this is my son, Stephen. Michael, unfortunately, couldn't come. He's away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Stephen, would you-- Stephen, would you like to say something, why you think it's important that your father made this video?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Dad is a survivor. But I think he's more than just a survivor. For me, he's a lighthouse, that is, a house of light, a house of enlightenment, and a light that shows the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mrs. Goddard would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a few words. I'm glad we've done that. It means a lot to my husband. And I know he always wanted to have that memo-- what is this? What is the word? [INAUDIBLE] No, oh. Sorry. I wanted to say that he wanted it-- documented-- documented. So I'm glad he's got that. And maybe now I'll have a bit of piece and quiet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
